article_title: Webb Spectrum of GLIMPSE-17775 Delivers Strongest Evidence Yet That 'Little Red Dots' Are Black Hole Stars
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Webb Spectrum of GLIMPSE-17775 Delivers Strongest Evidence Yet That 'Little Red Dots' Are Black Hole Stars
content_preview.summary: A 40-plus-line JWST spectrum of a gravitationally lensed little red dot, including a 16-line 'iron forest,' best fits a supermassive black hole cloaked in dense gas, astronomers report in The Astrophysical Journal.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers using NASA's James Webb Space Telescope report the strongest evidence to date that the mysterious "little red dots" scattered across the early universe are "black hole stars" — rapidly accreting supermassive black holes shrouded in dense cocoons of partially ionized gas. The conclusion rests on the deepest spectrum yet obtained of one such object, designated GLIMPSE-17775, and was published on June 10 in [The Astrophysical Journal](https://science.nasa.gov/missions/webb/...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (707 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis sections. Technical depth (electron-scattering broadening, the 16-line iron forest, the BH* scenario, X-ray faintness) is rendered accessibly without distortion. Appropriately hedges with a 'What We Don't Know' section noting the single-object, lensed-spectrum basis and the unresolved population-wide question.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read both available snapshots from disk. source-0.html.gz (science.nasa.gov, HTTP 200, primary): confirms verbatim every load-bearing claim and all three Kokorev quotes — designation 'GLIMPSE-17775', cluster 'Abell S1063', lead author 'Vasily Kokorev at the University of Texas at Austin', redshift 3.5 / ~1.8 billion years after the big bang, 30-hour spectrum equivalent to 80 hours via lensing, 'more than 40 spectral lines', the BH* (black hole star) dense-cocoon model, electron-scattering broadening as 'a telltale sign that a dense, layered gas cocoon is enshrouding this source', the 16 iron lines dubbed an 'iron forest' plus certain oxygen lines requiring a high-energy source, the X-ray-faintness explanation, and publication on June 10 (Jun 10, 2026) in The Astrophysical Journal. source-1.html.gz (space.com, HTTP 200): independently confirms 'GLIMPSE-17775', 'Abell S1063', and 'V. Kokorev (University of Texas at Austin)' — fully supporting the lead-author affiliation claim the article cites to Space.com. THIRD SOURCE phys.org returned HTTP 403 (bot-blocked); manifest file is null, so NO snapshot was archived and it could not be read. The three claims the article cites to phys.org (the 30/80-hour lensing equivalence, 'more than 40 spectral lines', and the X-ray-faintness explanation) are each independently confirmed verbatim in the NASA primary snapshot I personally read, so no claim rests solely on the unverifiable source. All three domains (science.nasa.gov, space.com, phys.org) are on the allowlist.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specifics trace to a cited, verified source. Designation, cluster name, redshift, author, affiliation, line counts, and quotes all match the NASA primary byte-accurately. No hallucinated quotes or fabricated specifics detected. Headline and lead rest entirely on the allowlisted NASA primary (HTTP 200, verified).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Attributes interpretive claims to NASA/the research team rather than asserting them; no sensationalism despite the 'strongest evidence yet' framing, which mirrors NASA's own headline language.
editor_notes.originality: No prior coverage of little red dots, GLIMPSE-17775, or black hole stars in the archive. The two other June 2026 JWST articles (Saturn rotation; WASP-121 b) are unrelated. Genuinely new topic.
concerns: The phys.org source (one of three) returned HTTP 403 and was not archived (snapshot file is null). It is therefore unverifiable from the provenance chain. This is the basis for the APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS verdict and the filed corrections record. Mitigation: every claim the article attributes to phys.org is independently confirmed verbatim in the NASA primary snapshot, which was successfully archived and read.
recommendations: Filing a corrections record noting that the phys.org source could not be archived (HTTP 403) and that its cited claims are independently corroborated by the NASA primary.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Accurate, neutral, well-sourced News article whose headline and lead are fully backed by the allowlisted NASA primary. Every quote and specific verified verbatim. The only blemish is that one of three corroborating sources (phys.org) was bot-blocked and could not be archived; since its claims are independently confirmed by the NASA primary, the issue is honestly recoverable via a corrections note. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
